original_hypothesis.title: AccessControlHooks.onQueueWithdrawal Missing isHooked Market Validation Allows Arbitrary Credential State Manipulation
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: AccessControlHooks.onQueueWithdrawal does not validate that msg.sender is a registered hooked market. Unlike onDeposit and onTransfer (which check _hookedMarkets[msg.sender].isHooked), and unlike FixedTermLoanHooks.onQueueWithdrawal (which has the check), this function can be called by any external contract. This triggers _tryValidateAccess, which writes to _lenderStatus storage and makes stateful external calls to role providers via _tryValidateCredential. An attacker can: (1) force-expire a lender's credential by calling when their credential has lapsed, causing unsetCredential to be written to storage; (2) trigger stateful validateCredential calls on providers via crafted hooksData, consuming one-time-use tokens or causing other side effects without the lender's consent; (3) grief lenders by manipulating their credential state before time-sensitive deposit or withdrawal operations.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a direct access control bypass. The FixedTermLoanHooks version has the isHooked check but AccessControlHooks does not, indicating the check was likely intended but omitted. The function modifies critical storage (_lenderStatus) and makes external stateful calls, making the impact high.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onQueueWithdrawal has no isHooked check on msg.sender; directly calls _tryValidateAccess which writes _lenderStatus to storage. Compare with FixedTermLoanHooks.onQueueWithdrawal which checks _hookedMarkets[msg.sender].isHooked at line entry.

preconditions: AccessControlHooks is deployed and has at least one registered role provider
Target lender has an existing _lenderStatus entry (e.g., expired or active credential)
Attacker can call onQueueWithdrawal from any address (no access restriction)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing market validation check that exists in peer functions and sibling contract
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Any address can call onQueueWithdrawal(victim, 0, 0, fakeState, maliciousHooksData) to trigger _tryValidateAccess → _tryValidateAccessInner → _writeLenderStatus, writing lender credential state to storage
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No _hookedMarkets[msg.sender].isHooked check (present in onDeposit, onTransfer, and FixedTermLoanHooks.onQueueWithdrawal)

analysis_questions: Can an attacker force-unset an active lender's credential by calling from an unregistered address?
Can the crafted hooksData trigger validateCredential on a provider with irreversible side effects?
Does isKnownLenderOnMarket[lender][attacker_address] always return false, guaranteeing _tryValidateAccess is always invoked?
Can this be chained with other hooks to prevent a lender from depositing or withdrawing?
validation_requirements: Deploy AccessControlHooks and call onQueueWithdrawal from a non-market address
Verify _lenderStatus is modified in storage after the call
Test with hooksData containing a real provider address to verify stateful call occurs
Compare behavior with FixedTermLoanHooks.onQueueWithdrawal to confirm the missing check

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deploys contract that calls AccessControlHooks.onQueueWithdrawal(victimLender, 0, 0, fakeMarketState, providerAddress+maliciousData). Since msg.sender is not a hooked market, isKnownLenderOnMarket is false, forcing _tryValidateAccess. If victim has expired credential, it gets unset from storage. If hooksData targets a stateful provider, validateCredential is called consuming the victim's credential token.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Lenders can be denied access to deposits or withdrawals by having credentials force-expired; one-time-use credentials can be burned without consent
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All lenders using AccessControlHooks on any market managed by this hooks instance
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Lender must re-authenticate with a provider, but one-time credentials may be permanently consumed

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying that onQueueWithdrawal rejects calls from non-hooked-market addresses

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time a target lender has a credential
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain event emission would show AccountAccessRevoked from unexpected context

validation_result.mechanism: Missing isHooked market validation in onQueueWithdrawal
validation_result.impact_summary: AccessControlHooks.onQueueWithdrawal can be called by any address, triggering _tryValidateAccess which makes stateful external calls to role providers and writes credential state to storage when valid credentials are found, bypassing the market authorization check present in all peer functions.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: AccessControlHooks.onQueueWithdrawal is missing the `_hookedMarkets[msg.sender].isHooked` check that is present in onDeposit (line ~492), onTransfer (line ~530), and FixedTermLoanHooks.onQueueWithdrawal (line ~473). Any external address can call this function with an arbitrary `lender` address and crafted `hooksData`. Since `isKnownLenderOnMarket[lender][attacker_address]` will be false for an attacker's address, `_tryValidateAccess` is always invoked. This leads to `_tryValidateAccessInner` which can: (1) make stateful CALL to providers via `_tryValidateCredential` when hooksData > 20 bytes, (2) make staticcalls to pull providers during credential refresh loops, and (3) write credential updates to `_lenderStatus` storage via `_writeLenderStatus`. When the credential validation fails, the outer function reverts with NotApprovedLender, rolling back all state changes. However, when validation succeeds (lender has a valid or refreshable credential), the function completes successfully, persisting credential state changes triggered by the unauthorized caller. This allows an attacker to force credential validation and state updates for any lender without the lender's consent or market authorization.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The defect is conclusively proven by code comparison. AccessControlHooks.onQueueWithdrawal at approximately line 490-500 has NO isHooked check, while onDeposit at ~line 462 and onTransfer at ~line 530 both check `if (!market.isHooked) revert NotHookedMarket()`. FixedTermLoanHooks.onQueueWithdrawal at ~line 473 also checks `if (!market.isHooked) revert NotHookedMarket()`. The omission is clearly unintentional.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Missing `HookedMarket memory market = _hookedMarkets[msg.sender]; if (!market.isHooked) revert NotHookedMarket();` check that is present in all peer functions (onDeposit, onTransfer) and in FixedTermLoanHooks.onQueueWithdrawal. Any address can call this function.
This is the correct implementation showing the isHooked check that AccessControlHooks is missing.
Shows that onDeposit in the same contract correctly checks isHooked, confirming the omission in onQueueWithdrawal is a defect.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls onQueueWithdrawal(victimLender, 0, 0, fakeState, crafted_hooksData) from any address
2. No isHooked check - function proceeds directly
4. isKnownLenderOnMarket[lender][attacker_address] returns false (attacker is not a market)
7. If hooksData > 20 bytes: _tryValidateCredential makes stateful CALL to provider.validateCredential(lender, data)
8. If credential refresh needed: _tryGetCredential makes staticcall to pull providers
9. _writeLenderStatus writes credential state to _lenderStatus[lender] if wasUpdated=true
10. If hasValidCredential=true: function succeeds, state changes persist
11. If hasValidCredential=false: function reverts with NotApprovedLender, state changes rolled back
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerable path is: onQueueWithdrawal → _tryValidateAccess → _tryValidateAccessInner → _handleHooksData → _tryValidateCredential. When an attacker provides hooksData with >20 bytes containing a valid provider address and credential data, _tryValidateCredential (AccessControlHooks.sol:~395) makes a stateful CALL to the provider's validateCredential function. If this returns a valid credential, _writeLenderStatus (AccessControlHooks.sol:~440) writes the updated credential to _lenderStatus storage. The function then returns true, and the outer onQueueWithdrawal succeeds. The key issue is that this entire flow is triggered without any verification that msg.sender is an authorized (hooked) market.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that are relevant to AccessControlHooks deployment configuration. LibDeployment.sol is a generic deployment utility.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is validated as follows: (1) Attacker deploys a contract or calls directly to AccessControlHooks.onQueueWithdrawal(victimLender, 0, 0, fakeMarketState, hooksData). (2) Since no isHooked check exists, the call proceeds. (3) isKnownLenderOnMarket[victimLender][attacker] is false, so _tryValidateAccess is invoked. (4a) If victim has a valid/refreshable credential and attacker provides appropriate hooksData, the function succeeds and the credential state is updated in storage (potentially changing the lastProvider or lastApprovalTimestamp). (4b) If hooksData triggers _tryValidateCredential, provider.validateCredential is called with CALL opcode, causing stateful side effects on the provider regardless of outcome. (4c) If victim has no valid credential, the function reverts, rolling back all state changes. The primary confirmed impact is unauthorized invocation of stateful provider calls and potential credential state manipulation for lenders with valid/refreshable credentials.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM-HIGH severity. The missing isHooked check is a clear code defect. While the revert-on-failure mechanism limits the most severe attack vectors (force-unsetting credentials is rolled back), the defect still enables: (1) unauthorized stateful calls to role providers via _tryValidateCredential, which could consume one-time credentials or cause provider-side state changes; (2) credential state updates for lenders with valid credentials, potentially changing their lastProvider; (3) grief attacks that force unnecessary provider interactions. The defect is consistently present only in AccessControlHooks, not FixedTermLoanHooks, confirming it is an accidental omission.
recommended_mitigations: Add the isHooked market validation at the beginning of AccessControlHooks.onQueueWithdrawal: `HookedMarket memory market = _hookedMarkets[msg.sender]; if (!market.isHooked) revert NotHookedMarket();`
Consider adding a `withdrawalRequiresAccess` field to AccessControlHooks.HookedMarket struct (as FixedTermLoanHooks has) to allow markets to optionally require access for withdrawals
Add comprehensive test coverage verifying that all hook entry points (onDeposit, onQueueWithdrawal, onTransfer) reject calls from non-hooked-market addresses
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy AccessControlHooks with a borrower. 2. Add a role provider. 3. Grant credential to a lender via grantRole. 4. Call onQueueWithdrawal from an unauthorized address (not a hooked market) with the lender's address and hooksData containing the provider address + credential data. 5. Verify the call does NOT revert with NotHookedMarket. 6. Verify _lenderStatus is updated or stateful provider calls are made. 7. Compare with FixedTermLoanHooks.onQueueWithdrawal which should revert with NotHookedMarket for the same unauthorized caller.
uncertainties: The severity of stateful provider calls depends on the specific provider implementations, which are external and not in scope

The revert-on-failure mechanism in the outer function limits some attack scenarios (credential unsetting), but the stateful nature of _tryValidateCredential means provider-side effects may still be a concern depending on provider design
Whether real-world providers use one-time credential tokens that could be burned by this attack
additional_testing_needed: Test calling onQueueWithdrawal from a non-hooked-market address and verify no isHooked revert occurs
Test with a mock stateful provider to verify validateCredential is called even from unauthorized callers
Test the complete flow where credential state is written to storage for a lender with a valid refreshable credential
Fuzz test to find edge cases where credential state is persistently modified via this path
